mappings.rationaleAI: Social domain of production, distribution, and consumption driven by natural resources, labor, and capital (A), with economic systems ranging from market to planned (C).
Social science encompassing basic economic concepts (A), microeconomic analysis (B.1), sectoral economics (B.2), macroeconomic analysis (B.3), and comparison of economic systems (C).

Individual decision-making on resource allocation addressing scarcity and choosing among resources (A.1), diminishing returns and optimization (A.3), marginality (A.4), competition (A.6), and microeconomic level of analysis (B.1).
Aggregate economic analysis of GDP, unemployment, and inflation constituting macroeconomics as the economy as a whole (B.3), with growth and development concepts (A.8).

Wealth of Nations covering division of labor (A.2), capital accumulation (A.5), free market competition (A.6), critique of mercantilism implying comparative advantage (A.7), and foundational to Western-type market economies (C.2).
Social ownership of means of production constituting the centrally planned socialist model (C.1.b) and Soviet-and socialist-type economic systems (C.3).
Foundational model of price determination addressing scarcity and resource choice (A.1), equilibrium through diminishing returns (A.3), and marginal analysis (A.4).
Productive activity with division of labor as a core analytical concept, directly constituting the concept of division of labor (A.2).
Organized profit-seeking activity operating through market competition (A.6) within Western-type market economies (C.2).
Private ownership with capital accumulation (A.5) and market competition (A.6), constituting the pure private enterprise economy model (C.1.a) and Western-type market economies (C.2).
Collective ownership and planned economy constituting the centrally planned socialist model (C.1.b) and Soviet-type economic systems (C.3).
Rights over tangible and intangible things relevant to the concept of capital (A.5) and the pure private enterprise economy based on private property (C.1.a).
Transfer of goods between parties through specialization, directly relevant to the concept of comparative advantage (A.7).
Critique of capital accumulation (A.5), theory of centrally planned economy (C.1.b), and foundational to Soviet-type economic systems (C.3).
Transformed the USSR through forced collectivization and five-year plans, directly exemplifying Soviet-type economic systems (C.3).
Discipline of monetary resource management directly relevant to the concept of capital and its allocation (A.5).
Lack of financial resources relevant to growth and development concepts (A.8) and mixed economies in developing countries (C.4).
Government-funded support systems constituting a feature of the mixed economy with various degrees of economic planning (C.1.c).
Typology from hunting-gathering through industrial societies relevant to primitive economic systems (C.5.a).
Period from invention of writing covering early civilizations, relevant to primitive economic systems and their historical context (C.5.a).
Severe global economic downturn illustrating macroeconomic analysis of aggregate output and employment (B.3) within the context of Western-type market economies (C.2).
Intensifying economic interdependence driven by comparative advantage in trade (A.7) and concepts of growth and development (A.8).
Process of colonial territories achieving independence, directly relevant to mixed economies in developing countries (C.4).
Island city-state with Four Asian Tigers economic transformation, exemplifying development trajectories of mixed economies in developing countries (C.4).
Largest Southeast Asian economy with post-independence development, representing mixed economies in developing countries (C.4).
Upper-middle-income Southeast Asian economy with strengths in electronics and petrochemicals, representing mixed economies in developing countries (C.4).
Region with contemporary economic dynamism, illustrating development trajectories of mixed economies (C.4).
